rows.title: Partnership on AI Case Study: Italian Union SLC-CGIL Secures Company-Level AI Agreement with Konecta Italia
rows.summary:
Partnership on AI has published a case study examining Italy's first company-level AI agreement, signed in March 2025 between business process outsourcing firm Konecta Italia and the communications union SLC-CGIL (Sindacato Lavoratori della Comunicazione), covering approximately 7,000 employees.

On 9 April 2026, Partnership on AI published the case study, based on interviews with senior representatives of SLC-CGIL, documenting how the agreement was negotiated and what protections it established for call centre workers subject to AI deployment.

Partnership on AI finds that the agreement was facilitated by Italy's existing labour policy framework, which includes sector-level bargaining provisions and laws requiring negotiation over the introduction of new technology tools. Partnership on AI notes that these preexisting legal structures gave SLC-CGIL meaningful leverage during negotiations, enabling the union to secure protections that would not have been readily achievable in jurisdictions with less prescriptive technology bargaining obligations. Partnership on AI finds that unions operating within comparable frameworks can use existing worker data protections as a foundation for new demands relating to AI training data collection and associated surveillance risks.

Partnership on AI determines that the agreement addresses short-term surveillance concerns by establishing safeguards governing how worker data is collected and used for AI training purposes. Partnership on AI further notes that the parties framed AI explicitly as a tool rather than a replacement for workers, a definitional approach which Partnership on AI finds establishes a basis for managing displacement risks within the governance structure of the agreement. Partnership on AI also finds, however, that longer-term risks, particularly those relating to generative AI, data use beyond initial training, and structural job loss, remain difficult to address through collective bargaining alone. Partnership on AI concludes that even where unions are unable to prevent the introduction of AI systems, participation in ongoing governance processes represents an achievable and documented outcome of negotiation, as illustrated by the terms secured by SLC-CGIL in this agreement.

rows.title: Partnership on AI Case Study: Pennsylvania Government and SEIU Local 668 Reach Binding AI Protections Agreement for State Employees
rows.summary:
The Partnership on AI has published a case study examining a binding agreement reached in March 2025 between the Commonwealth of Pennsylvania and SEIU Local 668, establishing AI protections for nearly 10,000 state employees and creating a formal labour-management collaboration structure for AI governance.

On 9 April 2026, the Partnership on AI published the case study detailing how Pennsylvania Governor Josh Shapiro issued a letter in March 2025 outlining eight AI protections and provisions for state employees, the majority of whom hold union-represented positions. Simultaneously, the Governor's Office of Administration negotiated a binding side letter agreement with SEIU Local 668, the full text of which appears as an annex to the case study.

The Partnership on AI finds that the side letter formally establishes a Generative AI labour and Management Collaboration Group, described in the case study as a mechanism for ongoing AI governance as technology evolves. The Partnership on AI notes that the agreement was reached through direct engagement with officials from the Pennsylvania Office of Administration and the president of SEIU Local 668, drawing on perspectives from both the employer and union sides of the negotiation. The Partnership on AI identifies that defining AI as a tool rather than a replacement for workers formed a foundational element of the negotiations, with the case study noting this framing as central to addressing displacement risks while enabling organisational innovation.

The Partnership on AI further finds that the agreement offers broader lessons for public sector AI governance. The case study identifies seven key insights, including the value of building on established labour-management trust, involving workers as subject matter experts in policy development, and pursuing high-level agreements capable of extending protections across multiple unions through what the case study terms patterning or impact bargaining. The Partnership on AI notes that SEIU Local 668's approach included proactive engagement even in instances where unions had not been formally invited to participate in AI discussions, and that the Commonwealth of Pennsylvania's leadership prioritised worker involvement from the outset to support accessible and efficient AI-supported public services.

rows.title: MLCommons Publishes Benchmarking Methodology for Jailbreak Attacks on Large Language Models
rows.summary:
MLCommons, in collaboration with researchers from institutions including the Alan Turing Institute, Google DeepMind, NVIDIA, Meta, and the Infocomm Media Development Authority of Singapore, has published a methodology paper proposing a taxonomy-driven framework for benchmarking single-turn jailbreak attacks on large language models, with reference to ISO/IEC 42001 as a governance alignment standard.

On 16 February 2026, MLCommons released the paper titled "A Robust, Defensible, and Reproducible Methodology for Benchmarking Single-Turn Jailbreak Attacks on Large Language Models", authored by a multi-institutional team across academia, industry and government bodies. The paper builds on prior MLCommons infrastructure, including AILuminate v1.0 and the MLCommons Jailbreak Benchmark v0.5, and addresses what the authors identify as structural limitations in existing jailbreak evaluation practice.

The paper finds that current jailbreak benchmarks exhibit three categories of deficiency. First, the paper finds that many benchmarks lack defensibility, as attack selection proceeds without a formal taxonomy, meaning external auditors cannot verify whether a benchmark meaningfully represents the relevant attack surface, which the paper states undermines governance credibility. Second, the paper finds that coverage gaps exist across attack families, noting that encoding-based obfuscation, semantic-preserving perturbations, and compositional instruction strategies are frequently underrepresented in evaluation corpora, leading to what the paper characterises as misleading robustness estimates. Third, the paper finds that reproducibility is compromised where categories overlap, operate at inconsistent abstraction levels, or lack deterministic placement rules, weakening inter-annotator agreement and making cross-organisational comparisons unstable.

In response to these findings, the paper introduces what it describes as a mechanism-first, benchmark-operational taxonomy of single-turn jailbreak attacks. The paper states that organisations deploying large language models in safety-critical, enterprise, and regulated environments require evaluation methodologies that are reproducible, interpretable, and defensible to auditors and governance bodies. The paper frames resistance to single-turn inference-time jailbreak attacks, defined as prompt manipulations designed to bypass safety controls, as an operational and governance requirement rather than a research concern, and positions the proposed taxonomy as a foundation for evaluations that can withstand regulatory scrutiny and support auditability in line with ISO/IEC 42001.

rows.title: OECD and European Commission Release Harmonised Framework for Measuring AI Investment Across EU and Comparator Economies
rows.summary:
The OECD and the European Commission have jointly published a harmonised methodology for quantifying artificial intelligence investment across the European Union and benchmarking it against the United States, the United Kingdom, Canada, and Japan, providing a standardised basis for evaluating national and regional AI strategies.

On 26 September 2025, the Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development and the European Commission released "Advancing the measurement of investments in artificial intelligence", a report establishing a two-step measurement framework designed to isolate AI-related spending from broader information and communications technology or research and development expenditure. The framework draws on macroeconomic data from Eurostat and the EUKLEMS and INTANProd databases, combined with AI intensity coefficients derived from patent data and education statistics.

The report defines AI investment broadly, encompassing not only research and development but also complementary assets including skills development, data infrastructure, computing hardware, and organisational capital. The framework notes that AI functions as a general-purpose technology whose economic footprint extends well beyond software development, and the methodology is constructed accordingly to capture this wider scope of spending.

Applying the framework to available data, the report finds that total AI investment across the EU27 reached approximately EUR 257 billion in 2023, with estimates ranging from EUR 220 billion to EUR 294 billion depending on assumptions applied. The report notes this figure largely met the EUR 20 billion annual investment target set under the European Union Coordinated Plan on Artificial Intelligence, which was launched in 2018 and revised in 2021 with a 2030 horizon. The framework finds that private investment accounts for 73 per cent of total AI spending, with skills development comprising the largest single category at 41 per cent, followed by data and equipment at 37 per cent, research and development at 13 per cent, and other intellectual property products at 9 per cent. The report identifies Germany, France, and the Netherlands as leading in overall investment, while Ireland ranks highest on a per capita basis. The framework also finds a notable acceleration in AI investment across all categories in the post-2019 period, particularly in software, development, and AI-enabling infrastructure. The European Commission concurrently launched its Apply AI Strategy, which incorporates a European AI Observatory intended to use the new measurement framework as one of its primary data sources for monitoring AI development and trends across sectors.

rows.title: Indonesia Adopts First Age-Appropriate Design Regulation in Asia, Drawing on IEEE Standards
rows.summary:
Indonesia has enacted a binding government regulation on the governance of electronic systems in child protection, drawing on frameworks developed with input from the IEEE Standards Association (IEEE SA), establishing enforceable requirements for digital platforms operating in the country.

On 14 May 2025, the IEEE Standards Association announced the culmination of its collaboration with Indonesian policymakers on the regulation, described by IEEE SA as the first age-appropriate design regulation of its kind in Asia and the Global South. No financial penalties are specified in the source material; the regulation establishes compliance obligations rather than a fining regime.

The regulation requires online service providers to prioritise the best interests of children in platform design and practices, building on the UN Convention on the Rights of the Child and its General Comment No. 25. The regulation provides that any digital product or service likely to be accessed by children is required to offer a high level of privacy by default. The regulation further prohibits the profiling and precise geolocation of children, and bans manipulative design practices, including nudging users to weaken privacy settings or collecting more personal data than is necessary for the service.

IEEE SA states that the regulation is closely aligned with the UK Age-Appropriate Design Code and California's equivalent framework. IEEE SA notes that the regulation incorporates provisions drawn from IEEE 2089, the IEEE Standard for Age Appropriate Digital Services Framework, and IEEE 2089.1, the IEEE Standard for Online Age Verification. IEEE SA notes that by embedding provisions from these standards, the regulation requires online services to follow internationally vetted guidelines for protecting children's data and online experiences. IEEE SA also states that it has launched a certification programme to assist industry in conforming to age verification best practices consistent with those standards.

IEEE SA states that children now represent approximately one third of all internet users worldwide, and that the regulatory engagement in Indonesia is intended to serve as a model for collaborative policy development between technical bodies, governments, and civil society. IEEE SA has separately announced the launch of a Technology Policy Collaborative to extend similar advisory engagement to other jurisdictions on digital governance matters.

rows.title: OECD AI Observatory Publishes Common Reporting Framework for AI Incidents
rows.summary:
The OECD AI Observatory has published a standardised framework for reporting AI incidents internationally, setting out a common structure for documenting AI-related harms across industries and jurisdictions, drawing on the Observatory's definition of AI incidents adopted in May 2024.

On 31 January 2025, the OECD AI Observatory released "Towards a Common Reporting Framework for AI Incidents", developed with guidance from the OECD Expert Group on AI Incidents, co-chaired by Marko Grobelnik, Mark Latonero, Irina Orssich, and Elham Tabassi. The publication does not impose fines or binding obligations and carries no financial penalty.

The Observatory defines an AI incident, as formalised in May 2024, as any event, circumstance, or series of events in which an AI system's development, use, or malfunction causes harm, either directly or indirectly. The framework identifies a range of incident types, including deepfake fraud, non-consensual AI-generated imagery, disinformation, biased algorithmic decision-making, and failures in automated systems. The Observatory notes that in 2023, France alone recorded over 130,000 online scams, representing an 8% increase from the prior year, and attributes part of this growth to AI-generated content and deepfake technology. Among the cases catalogued, the Observatory references a fraud incident in which a victim transferred 830,000 euros to perpetrators using deepfake videos and AI-generated images impersonating a well-known public figure.

The Observatory finds that AI-related harms do not arise solely from malicious actors. The publication identifies design flaws as a separate category of risk, citing a recruitment algorithm discontinued after it was found to discriminate against women, and a separate AI hiring system that resulted in a six-figure settlement following findings that it excluded women over 55 and men over 60. The Observatory also references a healthcare algorithm that the publication states unintentionally prioritised White patients over Black patients by linking care allocation to cost rather than medical urgency, noting that subsequent intervention reduced the measured bias by over 80%.

The Observatory states that the common framework is intended to be consistent yet flexible, enabling policymakers to align responses across diverse legal and regulatory environments. The Observatory's AI Incidents Monitor, described as a media-based tracking tool, is presented as a companion resource for analysing emerging incident patterns. The Observatory states that the framework identifies three intended outcomes for adopting the common structure: improved risk assessment through comparative incident data, faster identification of high-risk AI systems, and enhanced cross-border knowledge-sharing among regulators and industry participants.
